original_hypothesis.title: ServiceNft Impact Score Griefing via Public updateImpact
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `updateImpact` function in `ServiceNft` is public and lacks access control. When called after a service is minted, it compares the service's maturity against itself, resulting in a zero impact score and permanently destroying the contributor's reward allocation.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Impact scores determine the distribution of protocol rewards. Allowing any user to zero out these scores enables a griefing attack where attackers can deny rewards to legitimate contributors with no cost to themselves.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public function updates state based on `_coreServices` which is already set to the current `proposalId` if called after `mint`

preconditions: A service NFT has been minted with a positive impact score

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Calling after mint sets prevServiceId = proposalId, forcing rawImpact to 0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker destroys victim's reward share, proportionally increasing the reward share of all other participants (including the attacker)

economic_questions: Can an attacker automate this to zero out all competing contributions?
What is the exact financial gain for the attacker from the redistributed rewards?
validation_requirements: Test calling updateImpact immediately after mint

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to the victim's destroyed reward share
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI if attacker holds competing contributions

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a victim's ServiceNft is minted
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Destroys individual contributor value without affecting token price

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of intended rewards for that contribution
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All contributor rewards in the protocol

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if datasetImpactWeight affects the zeroing out

validation_result.mechanism: Public updateImpact self-comparison zeroes impact score
validation_result.impact_summary: Any user can call updateImpact() after a service NFT mint to zero out the impact score of any model contributor, permanently destroying their reward allocation until a new proposal passes governance.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The updateImpact function is public with no access control. During mint(), updateImpact is called BEFORE _coreServices is updated to the new proposalId. This means at mint time, the calculation correctly uses the previous service as reference. However, after mint() completes, _coreServices[virtualId][core] = proposalId. If anyone then calls updateImpact(virtualId, proposalId), prevServiceId resolves to proposalId itself. Since _maturities[proposalId] > _maturities[proposalId] is always false, rawImpact becomes 0, and _impacts[proposalId] is overwritten to 0. The dataset impact is also zeroed if applicable.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows: (1) updateImpact is public with no access modifier, (2) mint() sets _coreServices AFTER calling updateImpact, (3) updateImpact reads _coreServices to determine prevServiceId, (4) after mint, prevServiceId == proposalId causing self-comparison and zero impact.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: updateImpact is called BEFORE _coreServices is updated, so at mint-time the calculation is correct. But after mint completes, _coreServices points to the new proposalId, enabling the griefing attack.

Public function with no access control. After mint, _coreServices[virtualId][core] == proposalId, so prevServiceId == proposalId. The comparison _maturities[proposalId] > _maturities[proposalId] is always false, forcing rawImpact = 0 and overwriting _impacts[proposalId] = 0.

calling_sequence: 1. DAO executes proposal that calls ServiceNft.mint(virtualId, descHash)
2. mint() calls updateImpact(virtualId, proposalId) - correctly calculates impact using previous service
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: After ServiceNft.mint() completes for a model contribution, _coreServices[virtualId][core] is set to proposalId (ServiceNft.sol line ~77). Any caller can then invoke the public updateImpact(virtualId, proposalId) (ServiceNft.sol line 86). At line 87, prevServiceId = _coreServices[virtualId][_cores[proposalId]] resolves to proposalId. At lines 88-90, since _maturities[proposalId] is NOT greater than itself, rawImpact = 0. At line 93, _impacts[proposalId] = 0, overwriting the previously correct impact value.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. ServiceNft is deployed via upgradeable proxy (v1/deployService.ts) and ownership transferred to ADMIN, but updateImpact has no access control regardless of deployment config.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is fully validated: (1) Wait for a model service NFT to be minted via DAO proposal execution. (2) Call updateImpact(virtualId, proposalId) for the newly minted service. (3) The impact score is zeroed because _coreServices now points to proposalId itself. Cost: only gas for one transaction. The attack can be automated to front-run or immediately follow any service NFT mint transaction. The attacker can target any model contribution across any virtualId.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL - Any model contributor's impact score can be zeroed by anyone at near-zero cost. Impact scores determine protocol reward distribution. An attacker who holds competing contributions benefits from the redistributed rewards. The attack is repeatable for every new model service NFT mint, effectively denying rewards to all model contributors. Dataset impacts are also zeroed if the model has an associated dataset.
recommended_mitigations: Add access control to updateImpact: restrict to onlyOwner, or a specific role, or only callable from mint()
Make updateImpact internal instead of public, since it is only meant to be called during mint
If updateImpact must remain public for legitimate recalculation use cases, add a check: require(proposalId != _coreServices[virtualId][_cores[proposalId]], 'Cannot update current service')
Consider moving _coreServices assignment BEFORE updateImpact in mint() and adjusting the logic, or store previous service ID separately
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ServiceNft with ContributionNft and AgentNft mocks. 2. Set up a virtualId with a DAO. 3. Mint a first model service NFT (proposalId1) - this establishes baseline. 4. Mint a second model service NFT (proposalId2) with higher maturity - verify _impacts[proposalId2] > 0. 5. Call updateImpact(virtualId, proposalId2) from an unauthorized account. 6. Assert _impacts[proposalId2] == 0, confirming the griefing attack.
uncertainties: The exact downstream reward calculation that consumes _impacts is not fully visible in the provided code (likely in AgentRewardV2 which is not provided), but the zeroing of impact is confirmed
Whether the impact score can be restored by any means other than a new governance proposal passing
additional_testing_needed: Verify the exact reward distribution formula that depends on _impacts values
Test whether calling updateImpact multiple times after sequential mints can zero out all historical impacts
Test the dataset impact zeroing path when datasetId > 0
